a: The primeval history. God creates the heavens and earth and humanity in His image (1–2); Adam and Eve fall into sin and are expelled from Eden, with the first gospel promise given (3); Cain murders Abel and the genealogies of Cain and Seth unfold (4–5); the flood judges a corrupt world while Noah and his family are saved in the ark, followed by God's covenant with the rainbow sign (6–9); and the table of nations and the proud tower of Babel scatter humanity into languages and peoples (10–11), setting up the call of Abram.
summary: Creation (1–2), the fall (3), Cain and Seth (4–5), the flood and Noah (6–9), and the nations and Babel (10–11).

a: The Abraham narrative. God calls Abram from Ur and Haran and promises land, descendants, and blessing to the nations (12); Abram and Lot separate, and Abram rescues Lot and meets Melchizedek (13–14); God cuts covenant with Abram and gives the sign of circumcision (15, 17); Ishmael is born to Hagar (16); Sodom and Gomorrah are destroyed while Lot is spared (18–19); Isaac, the child of promise, is finally born to Sarah (21); Abraham's faith is tested in the binding of Isaac on Moriah (22); Sarah dies and is buried at Machpelah, and a wife, Rebekah, is sought for Isaac (23–24); Abraham dies (25).
summary: God calls Abraham and covenants with him, judges Sodom, gives Isaac, and tests Abraham at Moriah.

a: The Isaac and Jacob narratives. The covenant promises pass to Isaac (26); Jacob deceives Isaac to steal Esau's blessing and flees to Haran (27); at Bethel he sees the ladder and receives the promise (28); he marries Leah and Rachel and fathers eleven sons and a daughter while serving Laban (29–30); he departs from Laban and wrestles with God at Peniel, being renamed Israel (31–32); he reconciles with Esau (33); the violence at Shechem follows Dinah's defilement (34); God reaffirms the covenant at Bethel, Rachel dies bearing Benjamin, and Isaac dies (35); Esau's Edomite genealogy is recorded (36).
summary: Isaac's blessing, Jacob's deception and flight, his marriages and twelve sons, and his renaming as Israel.

a: The Joseph narrative (with the Judah–Tamar interlude in 38). Joseph's brothers sell him into Egypt out of jealousy (37); he resists Potiphar's wife, is imprisoned, and interprets dreams (39–40); he interprets Pharaoh's dreams and rises to govern Egypt during the famine (41); his brothers come for grain and are tested and reconciled, and Jacob's family moves to Goshen (42–47); Jacob blesses Joseph's sons and prophesies over his twelve sons, foretelling the scepter for Judah (48–49); Jacob dies and is buried in Canaan, and Joseph forgives his brothers and dies in Egypt (50).
summary: Joseph is sold to Egypt, rises to power, saves his family in famine, and Jacob blesses the twelve tribes.

a: Israel's bondage and deliverance from Egypt. The Israelites multiply and are enslaved under a new Pharaoh who orders the Hebrew boys killed (1); Moses is born, raised in Pharaoh's house, flees to Midian, and is called by God at the burning bush (2–4); Moses and Aaron confront Pharaoh, demanding release, and Pharaoh hardens his heart (5–6); God sends the plagues upon Egypt—blood, frogs, gnats, flies, livestock, boils, hail, locusts, and darkness (7–10); and the final plague on the firstborn is averted for Israel through the Passover lamb's blood, leading to the Exodus (11–12).
summary: Israel's slavery, Moses' call at the bush, the plagues on Egypt, and the Passover and Exodus.

a: From the Red Sea to Sinai and the covenant. God leads Israel by pillar of cloud and fire and parts the Red Sea, drowning Pharaoh's army, after which Moses and Miriam sing (13–15); in the wilderness God provides manna, quail, and water from the rock, and Israel battles Amalek (16–17); Jethro advises Moses to appoint judges (18); at Mount Sinai God descends in fire and gives the Ten Commandments (19–20); the Book of the Covenant lays out case laws on servants, restitution, and justice (21–23); and the covenant is ratified with blood as Moses, Aaron, and the elders eat before God (24).
summary: The Red Sea crossing, wilderness provision, the Ten Commandments, and the Sinai covenant ratified.

a: God's instructions for the tabernacle and priesthood. On the mountain God gives Moses the pattern for the sanctuary: the ark of the covenant, the table of showbread, and the golden lampstand (25); the tabernacle's curtains, boards, and veil (26); the bronze altar and the courtyard (27); the priestly garments, including the ephod and breastpiece, for Aaron and his sons (28); the consecration of the priests and the daily offerings (29); the altar of incense, the bronze laver, the anointing oil, and the incense (30); and the appointment of Bezalel and Oholiab as craftsmen, with a renewed command to keep the Sabbath (31).
summary: God gives the pattern for the tabernacle, its furnishings, the priestly garments, and Sabbath rest.

a: The golden calf, renewal of the covenant, and building of the tabernacle. While Moses is on the mountain, Israel makes a golden calf, provoking judgment and Moses' intercession (32); God threatens to withdraw His presence, and Moses pleads to see His glory (33); the covenant is renewed, the tablets remade, and Moses' face shines as he descends (34); the people give freely and skilled workers build the tabernacle, its furnishings, and the priestly garments exactly as commanded (35–39); and Moses erects the tabernacle, whereupon the glory of the LORD fills it and the cloud guides Israel's journeys (40).
summary: Israel's golden calf, Moses' intercession, the covenant renewed, and the tabernacle filled with God's glory.

a: The laws of the offerings. These chapters detail the five principal sacrifices Israel was to bring to the LORD at the tabernacle: the burnt offering, wholly consumed as atonement and devotion (1); the grain offering of fine flour, oil, and frankincense (2); the peace (fellowship) offering shared as a communal meal (3); the sin (purification) offering for unintentional sins (4); the guilt (trespass) offering with restitution for wrongs against God and neighbor (5–6); and additional regulations governing the priests' portions and the handling of each sacrifice (6–7). Together they form the sacrificial system that pointed forward to Christ.
summary: The regulations for the burnt, grain, peace, sin, and guilt offerings of Israel's sacrificial system.

a: The priesthood and the laws of clean and unclean. Aaron and his sons are ordained and consecrated to the priesthood (8); the tabernacle worship is inaugurated and fire from the LORD consumes the offering (9); Nadab and Abihu are struck dead for offering unauthorized fire (10); the dietary laws distinguish clean and unclean animals (11); laws govern purification after childbirth (12); the diagnosis and cleansing of skin diseases and mildew in garments and houses are detailed (13–14); and laws address bodily discharges that render a person ceremonially unclean (15).
summary: The priests are ordained, Nadab and Abihu judged, and laws of clean and unclean food and bodies given.

a: The Day of Atonement and the Holiness Code. The annual Day of Atonement provides cleansing for the nation through the sacrifice and the scapegoat sent into the wilderness (16); the law requires that all sacrifice be brought to the tabernacle and forbids eating blood, since the life is in the blood (17); laws forbid the sexual immorality of the surrounding nations (18); the call to be holy as God is holy governs love of neighbor and just dealing (19); penalties are set for idolatry and grave sins (20); and special requirements of holiness for the priests and the offerings they handle are given (21–22).
summary: The Day of Atonement and the Holiness Code, calling Israel to be holy as the LORD is holy.

a: The appointed feasts, sacred times, and covenant blessings and curses. God appoints Israel's feasts—Sabbath, Passover and Unleavened Bread, Firstfruits, Weeks (Pentecost), Trumpets, the Day of Atonement, and Booths (23); laws govern the lampstand, showbread, and the punishment of blasphemy (24); the Sabbath year and the Jubilee provide for rest of the land and release of debts and slaves (25); blessings for obedience and severe curses for disobedience are set before Israel (26); and laws conclude with vows, dedications, tithes, and redemption of things devoted to the LORD (27).
summary: Israel's appointed feasts, the Sabbath year and Jubilee, covenant blessings and curses, and vows.

a: Preparations at Sinai for the journey to Canaan. The first census numbers the fighting men of the twelve tribes (1); the camp is arranged by tribe around the tabernacle (2); the Levites are set apart and counted for service (3–4); laws address purity, restitution, the test for jealousy, and the Nazirite vow (5–6); the Aaronic blessing is given (6); the tribal leaders bring offerings for the altar's dedication (7); the Levites are consecrated and the second Passover kept (8–9); and at God's command, signaled by the cloud and silver trumpets, Israel sets out from Sinai (9–10).
summary: The census, ordering of the camp, the Levites' service, the Aaronic blessing, and departure from Sinai.

a: Israel's rebellions in the wilderness. The people complain and crave meat, and seventy elders receive the Spirit (11); Miriam and Aaron oppose Moses, and Miriam is struck with leprosy (12); twelve spies explore Canaan, but ten bring a fearful report, so Israel refuses to enter and is sentenced to forty years' wandering (13–14); various laws and Korah's rebellion against Moses and Aaron's authority follow, ending in judgment and Aaron's budding staff (15–17); the duties and provision of priests and Levites are confirmed and the red heifer ordinance given (18–19); and at Meribah Moses strikes the rock in disobedience, and Aaron dies (20).
summary: Israel's complaints, the spies' bad report and forty years' wandering, Korah's rebellion, and Meribah.

a: The journey toward the plains of Moab and preparation for Canaan. Israel defeats Sihon and Og and the bronze serpent heals the snake-bitten (21); Balak hires Balaam, who can only bless Israel, though his counsel later leads to sin at Peor (22–25); a second census numbers the new generation (26); Joshua is appointed Moses' successor (27); laws on offerings, festivals, and vows are given (28–30); Midian is judged and the tribes east of the Jordan settle (31–32); the journey stages are recorded and the land's boundaries and cities of refuge set (33–35); and laws on female inheritance through Zelophehad's daughters conclude the book (36).
summary: The bronze serpent, Balaam's oracles, the second census, Joshua's appointment, and laws for entering Canaan.

a: Moses' first address: historical review and the call to love God. Moses recounts Israel's journey from Horeb, the appointment of leaders, the failure of the spies, and the wilderness years (1–3); he urges obedience to God's law, warns against idolatry, and sets apart cities of refuge (4); he restates the Ten Commandments and the covenant at Horeb (5); he gives the Shema, the central command to love the LORD with all one's heart, soul, and might, and to teach it to the children (6); and he calls Israel to drive out the nations, remember God's faithfulness in the wilderness, and choose blessing through obedience (7–11).
summary: Moses reviews the journey, restates the Ten Commandments, and gives the Shema calling Israel to love God.

a: Moses' second address: the detailed laws of the covenant. These chapters expound how Israel is to live in the land: worship at the place God chooses and avoid false prophets and idolatry (12–13); clean and unclean foods, tithes, and the sabbatical release of debts (14–15); the three pilgrimage feasts and the appointment of judges, kings, priests, and prophets (16–18); cities of refuge, witnesses, and laws of warfare (19–21); various social, family, and purity laws protecting the weak and ordering daily life (22–25); and the offering of firstfruits and tithes with a confession of God's redemption (26).
summary: The detailed covenant laws on worship, feasts, leaders, justice, and care for the weak in the land.

a: Covenant blessings and curses, renewal, and the death of Moses. Moses commands the blessings and curses to be pronounced from Mounts Gerizim and Ebal (27); he sets out the blessings for obedience and the severe curses, including exile, for disobedience (28); the covenant is renewed in Moab, with the call to choose life (29–30); Moses commissions Joshua, writes the law, and gives his Song as a witness against Israel (31–32); he blesses the twelve tribes (33); and Moses views the Promised Land from Mount Nebo and dies, mourned by Israel, with Joshua taking up leadership (34).
summary: The blessings and curses, covenant renewal, Moses' song and blessing, and his death on Mount Nebo.
